A note to this shuffle. As python dicts and sets are not ordered, currently it will work properly without this shuffle (it will basically be shuffled by _create_fetch_requests, as it uses sets and dicts), but I still prefere it here, as:

* Python3.6 has ordered dicts, which can make the distribution way less equal
* While dicts are not retain insert order, they preserve key order by hashes, which can (probably) result in unequal consumption.

Please correct me if I'm wrong here. My tests run OK even without this shuffle, so I'm a bit concerned.

Are you sure about this specifically for ``contextmanager``? The [docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextlib.html) don't mention it and in my tests it is needed otherwise the post yield code is never reached. Testing with these snippets:
```python
from contextlib import contextmanager

things = []

@contextmanager
def TrySomething(value):
    things.append(value)
#     try:
    yield things[-1]
#     finally:
    things.pop() 
```
then
```python
with TrySomething('hello'):
    raise ValueError
```
gives  ``things=['hello']`` without the try/finally but ``[]`` with.

I spent a lot of time spinning my wheels on this.  As we found out, I didn't know the "decorator" jargon when you mentioned it, but I had already figured out what the @ stuff was doing functionally.

Also, I found this to be an odd result, because I thought the @spiceFoundExceptionThrower decorator checks the type of last item of the return (res[-1]) and should do nothing it that last item is not a [True/False] bool.  So summat odd is happening there - no wait, it _only_ checks type for the found==False case, so this is a bug, and the decorator should use `elif isinstance(found,bool)` instead of just `else`.

Wait a minute:  couldn't we change
`if isinstance(found, bool) and not found:` and `else:`
to simply
`if found is False:` and `elif found is True:`
in the spiceFoundExceptionThrower decorator?

Wouldn't a better (in the sense of more robust) decorator be
```
def spiceFoundExceptionThrower(f)
    @functools.wrapps(f)
    def wrapper(*args,**kwargs):
        res = f(*args,**kwargs):
        if not isinstance(res,tuple):   ### <== the A.R. side of me wants to do this
            return res
        found = res[-1]                 ### <== aack!
        if found is False:
            raise ...
        elif found is True:
            if len(res) == 2:
                return res[0]
            else:
                return res[:-1]
        return res
```
I'm still bothered by the possibility that the highlighted line (cf. [aack!] above) could throw an error (`found = ((None,)+res[-1:])[-1]`) would be more robust), but presumably that is taken care of by the choice of decorated functions.

So the input we are ignoring is actually a stealth input. Essentially, when someone sends a certain amount of coin to a stealth address, those coins get "burned". They get removed from the public ledger and added to the private one.

The reverse also happens when sending coins from a private address to a normal address. They essentially get minted in the public ledger. For these types of transactions, there is no ```prev_index``` to point to. Electrumx being a public index, I think it's safe to ignore these inputs, and only count any public outputs. This also happens in the reverse case. We ignore "invalid" (OP_RETURN) outputs, when a normal input sends coins to a stealth address.

Here is a high level (stratospheric) explanation of what happens:

https://github.com/tokenpay/tokenpay/blob/master/doc/Ring%20Signatures.md

hey thanks a lot for spotting this. I actually harbor this question for a long time: are hooks supposed to house the method that we use? or are hooks supposed to just assist we generate whatever client we need by getting parameters via Connections? 

From the documentation and code I have read so far, I'm observing two different perspectively. For example: 

- From the [airflow documentation](https://airflow.incubator.apache.org/concepts.html), we have: 

> Hooks are interfaces to external platforms and databases like Hive, S3, MySQL, Postgres, HDFS, and Pig. 

This means that the methods should be contained within the hook instance. We should follow the multi-inheritance implementation in this PR. 

- However, the implementation of [S3 hook](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/hooks/S3_hook.py) is more of a mixture. We do have `get_conn` meethod, while having some house keeping methods like `check_for_key`. 

- Finally, `get_conn` method might not serve us well if we have long running connections like RabbitMQ. For RabbitMQ, we might wanna establish a connection and only terminate it when all transactions are done. 

In sum the question I wanna ask is: what is a clear guideline on when to have the method persisted within the hook, and when should we separate out to another function?

`nodeChannel->mRotationKeys` is represented as a quaternion internally to assimp. That is `C_STRUCT aiQuaternion`. However when it is exported we need it as a simple vector of length 4.

`rotationData` is only used to export the `mRotationKeys` to the buffer. During this export into the buffer, it issues the [] operator which does not exist for the `aiQuaternion` struct.

This also makes sure that it is output to the buffer in the order of `x, y, z, w` which is the glTF specification.

> By the same token: Why not require the attribute keys/values to be "text strings", i.e. sequence of unicode code points?

I am doing that.

> Actually, to the extent that we're just blindly propagating them into the proto library, the real question is how the protobuf generated code handles unicode/str-style conversions, and whether we want to impose stricter semantics in the name of interplatform operability / uniqueness.

> The flip side is of course that if we do require strictly inputs of `six.text_type`, Python 2 users no longer have automagic support for publishing ascii raw strings (as valid ascii is valid utf-8 by definition). Open question as to whether this trade is worthwhile.

I think it is pretty reasonable to tell Python 2 users that they have to send `unicode` objects. The issue here is really realizing what you are sending and why. The fact that an ASCII-only binary string would _work_ is true, but part of the point that has been driven home to me by the CPS team is that users need to be explicitly aware of what they are sending. Being stringent on typing does this.

> At minimum, the comment ("These may be text strings or byte strings") is incorrect. While they are conceptually text strings, we will accept either the string or the UTF-8 encoded bytes thereof. Critically, this is not arbitrary binary (unlike the payload).

Fair point.

Python threads can run at the same time on different processors, as long as at most *one* is holding the GIL.  Long-running webapp processes, for instance, make heavy use of that fact -- they are written to release it when using their ORMs / persistence machinery.

It is even possible to do computationally-intensive work in parallel, assuming that the C extensions being used know to release the GIL after pulling needed data from Python objects.

It's not like it's happening suddenly with this PR, but this whole script is becoming very brittle, with state changing everywhere and all of it global. It is difficult to reason about. And it's error prone: sure, we update VENV_BIN here, but we don't update VENV_PATH. But that's okay because all the subsequent uses of it are wrapped in `if [ "$LE_AUTO_VERSION" != "$INSTALLED_VERSION" ]`, which should always be true in this case. *For now.* The assumptions are unstated and invisible, and they keep piling up. As we just talked about on IRC, we should do something about that.

And I *think* VENV_PATH is intended to mean "The modern venv path, as opposed to the OLD_VENV_PATH" vs. "The venv that we're going to run stuff from." I think we should comment that explicitly where VENV_PATH is first initialized, in this PR. That will help hold the semantics together until we do a cleanup job.
